From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Music produced without commercial record labels

"Indie music" redirects here. For other uses, see [Indie music (disambiguation)](/wiki/Indie_music_(disambiguation) "Indie music (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/LiquidMolly%27s_home_recording_studio_-_overview.jpg/250px-LiquidMolly%27s_home_recording_studio_-_overview.jpg)](/wiki/File:LiquidMolly%27s_home_recording_studio_-_overview.jpg)

Indie music is known for its [DIY](/wiki/Do_it_yourself "Do it yourself"), low-budget approach to music.

**Independent music** (also commonly known as **indie music**, or simply **indie**) is a broad style of music characterized by creative freedom, low budgets, and a [do-it-yourself](/wiki/Do_it_yourself "Do it yourself") approach to music creation. It originated from the liberties afforded by [independent record labels](/wiki/Independent_record_label "Independent record label"). Indie music describes a number of related styles, but generally refers to guitar-oriented music that deviates from mainstream conventions. There are a number of [subgenres](/wiki/Music_subgenre "Music subgenre") of independent music which combine its characteristics with other genres, such as [indie pop](/wiki/Indie_pop "Indie pop"), [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock"), [indie folk](/wiki/Indie_folk "Indie folk"), and [indie electronic](/wiki/Electronic_music#Indie_electronic "Electronic music"). Additionally, in certain circles, the term indie has taken on a definition entirely based on the typical sound of independent music in the 1980s, losing its connection to production style.

The origins of independent music lie in the early distribution of private press albums from the 1960s–1970s[[1]](#cite_note-1) as well as late '70s British independent record labels, from the early alternative music scene such as [Rough Trade](/wiki/Rough_Trade_Records "Rough Trade Records"), [Factory](/wiki/Factory_Records "Factory Records"), [Industrial Records](/wiki/Industrial_Records "Industrial Records") and [Mute](/wiki/Mute_Records "Mute Records"), which later contributed to the development of [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") music. *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* released the influential compilation album *[C86](/wiki/C86 "C86")* in 1986, which helped define [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock"). American independent music first emerged in the 1980s, and was spread via [college radios](/wiki/Campus_radio "Campus radio"), which led to the term [college rock](/wiki/College_rock "College rock"). Styles that evolved out of indie music and reached wide commercial success in the 1990s include [grunge](/wiki/Grunge "Grunge") ([Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)"), [Pearl Jam](/wiki/Pearl_Jam "Pearl Jam"), and [the Smashing Pumpkins](/wiki/The_Smashing_Pumpkins "The Smashing Pumpkins")) and [Britpop](/wiki/Britpop "Britpop") ([Blur](/wiki/Blur_(band) "Blur (band)"), [Pulp](/wiki/Pulp_(band) "Pulp (band)"), and [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)")). In the 21st century, due to the internet, indie music saw a global spread in popularity, as music fans were no longer dependent on physical publications to find new music.

Independent record labels, important to the development of indie music, are characterized by their smaller operations, lower funding, and greater creative control as compared to [major labels](/wiki/Record_label "Record label"). Independent labels use a variety of methods of distribution, with the label generally owning the copyright for the sound recording. They generally give smaller [advances](/wiki/Advance_against_royalties "Advance against royalties"), or sometimes no advance, and some may offer higher royalty splits than major labels.

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

Although "indie" was first used to described music released on independent record labels, the term grew to describe a specific sound because of the creative freedom of its initial bands and artists. A defining characteristic of indie music is that artists retain much more creative control over their music as compared to major labels.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Bands often have small budgets, and employ a [do-it-yourself](/wiki/Do_it_yourself "Do it yourself") ethos which influences their sound.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3) Indie music generally represents guitar-oriented music which strays away from commercial conventions. It often features lyrics that are earnest and emotive, with many cultural and sociopolitical references.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Many artists signed to major labels have retained creative control and are still considered indie artists.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Indie pop § Development and characteristics](/wiki/Indie_pop#Development_and_characteristics "Indie pop"), [Indie rock § History](/wiki/Indie_rock#History "Indie rock"), and [Lo-fi music § History](/wiki/Lo-fi_music#History "Lo-fi music")

### Origins of independent labels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origins of independent labels")]

[Independent labels](/wiki/Independent_record_label "Independent record label") have a long history of promoting developments in popular music, stretching back to the post-war period in the United States, particularly in genres overlooked by major labels.

During the 1940s–1950s, labels such as [King Records](/wiki/King_Records_(USA) "King Records (USA)"), [Sun Records](/wiki/Sun_Records "Sun Records"), and [Stax](/wiki/Stax_Records "Stax Records")[[4]](#cite_note-Rogan-4) played a crucial role in the development of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"), and early [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"), which were initially sidelined by the majors and were also responsible for pioneering both musical innovation and production techniques that major labels would later emulate, with [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records") being the first label to make recordings in stereo, while [Sun](/wiki/Sun_Records "Sun Records") and [Chess](/wiki/Chess_Records "Chess Records") introduced [slapback echo](/wiki/Slapback_echo "Slapback echo") and makeshift [echo chambers](/wiki/Echo_chamber "Echo chamber").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Additionally, independent labels were often the only platforms available for marginalized artists in America at the time, which included many Black musicians, as viable mediums to release and distribute their work.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

By the 1960s, several British producers and artists launched independent labels as outlets for their work and artists they liked[[4]](#cite_note-Rogan-4) [Joe Meek](/wiki/Joe_Meek "Joe Meek") ([Triumph Records](/wiki/Triumph_Records_(United_Kingdom) "Triumph Records (United Kingdom)")), [Andrew Loog Oldham](/wiki/Andrew_Loog_Oldham "Andrew Loog Oldham") ([Immediate Records](/wiki/Immediate_Records "Immediate Records")), [Chris Wright](/wiki/Chris_Wright_(music_industry_executive) "Chris Wright (music industry executive)") with [Terry Ellis](/wiki/Terry_Ellis_(record_producer) "Terry Ellis (record producer)") ([Chrysalis Records](/wiki/Chrysalis_Records "Chrysalis Records")) and [Larry Page](/wiki/Larry_Page_(British_singer_and_manager) "Larry Page (British singer and manager)") ([Page One Records](/wiki/Page_One_Records "Page One Records")).[[7]](#cite_note-Rogan2-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Independent labels gained further prominence in the American 1960s [underground music scene](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s") such as [ESP-Disk](/wiki/ESP-Disk "ESP-Disk") and [International Artists](/wiki/International_Artists "International Artists").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Other independent labels included those in Germany's [krautrock scene](/wiki/Krautrock "Krautrock") like [Kraftwerk](/wiki/Kraftwerk "Kraftwerk")'s own label [Kling Klang Records](/wiki/Kling_Klang_Records "Kling Klang Records") as well as [Ohr](/wiki/Ohr_Records "Ohr Records"), Brain,[[10]](#cite_note-10) and [Sky Records](/wiki/Sky_Records "Sky Records").

Prior to the late 1970s, major record companies held so much power that independent labels struggled to establish themselves. During this time, some popular artists formed their own labels, such as [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles")' [Apple Records](/wiki/Apple_Records "Apple Records"), [Frank Zappa](/wiki/Frank_Zappa "Frank Zappa")'s [Straight](/wiki/Straight_Records "Straight Records") and [Bizarre Records](/wiki/Bizarre_Records "Bizarre Records"), as well as [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones")' [Rolling Stones Records](/wiki/Rolling_Stones_Records "Rolling Stones Records") and [Grateful Dead](/wiki/Grateful_Dead "Grateful Dead")'s [Grateful Dead Records](/wiki/Grateful_Dead_Records "Grateful Dead Records"). However, these ventures often failed commercially or were eventually absorbed by major labels, until the launch of new labels like [Virgin Records](/wiki/Virgin_Records "Virgin Records").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

However, this dynamic began to change in 1979 when [Rough Trade](/wiki/Rough_Trade_Records "Rough Trade Records") released the album *[Inflammable Material](/wiki/Inflammable_Material "Inflammable Material")* by [Stiff Little Fingers](/wiki/Stiff_Little_Fingers "Stiff Little Fingers") which went on to be the first independently-released album to sell over 100,000 copies. This success sparked major record companies' interest in independent music and by the end of the decade, the establishment of the [UK indie charts](/wiki/UK_Independent_Singles_and_Albums_Charts "UK Independent Singles and Albums Charts") signaled the growing popularity of the movement.[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") documentary *Do it Yourself: The Story of Rough Trade* stated that "when [Rough Trade](/wiki/Rough_Trade_Records "Rough Trade Records") began in 1976 there were about a dozen independent labels in Britain, by the end of the decade there were over 800."[[13]](#cite_note-13) Other notable early indie labels include [Mute](/wiki/Mute_Records "Mute Records"), [4AD](/wiki/4AD "4AD"), [Factory](/wiki/Factory_Records "Factory Records") and [Creation Records](/wiki/Creation_Records "Creation Records").[[14]](#cite_note-:13-14)

### Emergence as a style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Emergence as a style")]

See also: [Indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") and [Indie pop](/wiki/Indie_pop "Indie pop")

During the [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") era, the number of independent labels grew as they became integral to the early years of punk rock musical distribution, as seen with [Beserkley Records](/wiki/Beserkley_Records "Beserkley Records") in the US, who put out the [debut album](/wiki/The_Modern_Lovers_(album) "The Modern Lovers (album)") of [the Modern Lovers](/wiki/The_Modern_Lovers "The Modern Lovers") which was recorded years earlier.[[4]](#cite_note-Rogan-4) In the UK, independent label [Stiff Records](/wiki/Stiff_Records "Stiff Records") released the first UK punk single "[New Rose](/wiki/New_Rose "New Rose")" by [the Damned](/wiki/The_Damned_(band) "The Damned (band)"). In [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), Brisbane band [the Saints](/wiki/The_Saints_(Australian_band) "The Saints (Australian band)") had their first punk release outside the US, "[(I'm) Stranded](/wiki/(I%27m)_Stranded_(song) "(I'm) Stranded (song)")", on their own "Fatal Records" label.[[15]](#cite_note-Lazell3-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

By 1977, [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester")-band [Buzzcocks](/wiki/Buzzcocks "Buzzcocks") released *[Spiral Scratch](/wiki/Spiral_Scratch "Spiral Scratch")*, which alongside [the Desperate Bicycles](/wiki/The_Desperate_Bicycles "The Desperate Bicycles") early singles showed listeners how to produce and distribute their own records independently at very low cost,[[17]](#cite_note-17) inspiring a wave of [DIY punk](/wiki/DIY_punk_ethic "DIY punk ethic") bands like [Swell Maps](/wiki/Swell_Maps "Swell Maps"), ['O' Level](/wiki/%27O%27_Level "'O' Level"), and [Television Personalities](/wiki/Television_Personalities "Television Personalities") who helped popularize independent rock releases.[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Distribution was further improved with the establishment of 'The Cartel', an association of companies like [Red Rhino](/wiki/Red_Rhino "Red Rhino") and [Rough Trade Records](/wiki/Rough_Trade_Records "Rough Trade Records") who would take the releases from these small labels and get them into [record shops](/wiki/Record_stores "Record stores") nationwide.[[15]](#cite_note-Lazell3-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/The_Smiths_%281984_Sire_publicity_photo%29_002.jpg/330px-The_Smiths_%281984_Sire_publicity_photo%29_002.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Smiths_(1984_Sire_publicity_photo)_002.jpg)

[Indie pop](/wiki/Indie_pop "Indie pop") band [the Smiths](/wiki/The_Smiths "The Smiths") in 1985

In the late 1970s, certain UK independent labels (such as [Rough Trade](/wiki/Rough_Trade_Records "Rough Trade Records"), [Factory](/wiki/Factory_Records "Factory Records"), [Fiction](/wiki/Fiction_Records "Fiction Records"), and [Mute](/wiki/Mute_Records "Mute Records")) contributed to the emergence of a distinct musical style found in indie music, which was influenced by [post-punk](/wiki/Post-punk "Post-punk") and [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music"). Important albums that contributed to this style include [Joy Division](/wiki/Joy_Division "Joy Division")'s *[Unknown Pleasures](/wiki/Unknown_Pleasures "Unknown Pleasures")* (1979) and [Depeche Mode](/wiki/Depeche_Mode "Depeche Mode")'s *[Speak & Spell](/wiki/Speak_%26_Spell_(album) "Speak & Spell (album)")* (1981).[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Released on Rough Trade, *[Inflammable Material](/wiki/Inflammable_Material "Inflammable Material")* (1979) was the first independently-released album to sell over 100,000 copies.[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) By the 1980s, the [indie pop](/wiki/Indie_pop "Indie pop") band [the Smiths](/wiki/The_Smiths "The Smiths"), signed with Rough Trade, "came to exemplify indie both musically and culturally" according to [*The Conversation*](/wiki/The_Conversation_(website) "The Conversation (website)"). The Smith's authentic sound contrasted with the common highly produced [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") of the time.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

The [UK Indie Chart](/wiki/UK_Indie_Chart "UK Indie Chart") was first compiled in 1980, and independent distribution became better organized from the late 1970s onward.[[15]](#cite_note-Lazell3-15) In 1986, *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* released the compilation album *[C86](/wiki/C86 "C86")*, which was influential to the development of indie music.[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12)[[19]](#cite_note-19) In the United States, independent music was first spread by in the 1980s by [college radios](/wiki/Campus_radio "Campus radio") and thus dubbed [college rock](/wiki/College_rock "College rock") (also later termed [modern rock](/wiki/Modern_rock "Modern rock") and [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock")).[[20]](#cite_note-:1-20) Defining American albums of this era include [Sonic Youth](/wiki/Sonic_Youth "Sonic Youth")'s *[Daydream Nation](/wiki/Daydream_Nation "Daydream Nation")* (1988) and [Pixies](/wiki/Pixies_(band) "Pixies (band)")' [*Doolittle*](/wiki/Doolittle_(album) "Doolittle (album)") (1989).[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12)

However, unlike the UK, this music was not referred to as "indie" until the 2000s.[[20]](#cite_note-:1-20) Indie music reached wide commercial success in the 1990s, especially with [Britpop](/wiki/Britpop "Britpop") bands like [Blur](/wiki/Blur_(band) "Blur (band)"), [Pulp](/wiki/Pulp_(band) "Pulp (band)"), and [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) As well, American grunge bands like [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)"), [Pearl Jam](/wiki/Pearl_Jam "Pearl Jam"), and [the Smashing Pumpkins](/wiki/The_Smashing_Pumpkins "The Smashing Pumpkins") received mainstream success.[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) In 1991, the [Grammys](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") added an [Alternative section](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Alternative_Music_Album "Grammy Award for Best Alternative Music Album") to its awards ceremony, for "non-traditional form[s]" existing "outside of the mainstream music consciousness".[[20]](#cite_note-:1-20)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Lorde_%282022%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Lorde_%282022%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lorde_(2022)_(cropped).jpg)

[Alt-pop](/wiki/Alt-pop "Alt-pop") musician [Lorde](/wiki/Lorde "Lorde") in 2022

The [internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet")'s ease of spreading information influenced indie music's popularization in the United States and global spread. Music fans no longer relied on publications or magazines to hear about new artists.[[20]](#cite_note-:1-20) At the beginning of the 21st century, the term indie came to describe a number of related but distinct subgenres. One example is [indie folk](/wiki/Indie_folk "Indie folk"), a stripped-back [low fidelity](/wiki/Lo-fi_music "Lo-fi music") approach to [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), as seen in [Fleet Foxes](/wiki/Fleet_Foxes "Fleet Foxes") or [Bon Iver](/wiki/Bon_Iver "Bon Iver")'s first album, *[For Emma, Forever Ago](/wiki/For_Emma,_Forever_Ago "For Emma, Forever Ago")*. Widely popular [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") bands of this era include [Arcade Fire](/wiki/Arcade_Fire "Arcade Fire") and [Arctic Monkeys](/wiki/Arctic_Monkeys "Arctic Monkeys").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) By this time, the term indie had transcended the definition of solely independently released music, and came to represent a "general resistance to popular and mainstream culture, evoking realism, independence and authenticity".[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Independent record labels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Independent record labels")]

Main article: [Independent record label](/wiki/Independent_record_label "Independent record label")

An [independent record label](/wiki/Independent_record_label "Independent record label") is one that operates outside of the funding and creative control of [major record labels](/wiki/Record_label#Major_labels "Record label"). Independent labels generally have greater creative freedom, at the cost of smaller budgets and personnel.[[21]](#cite_note-21) They are often able to support artists working in niche styles of music,[[22]](#cite_note-:02-22) and rely heavily on personal networking, or [word of mouth](/wiki/Word_of_mouth "Word of mouth"), to expose their acts.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Indie labels are usually small operations, with almost no outside assistance and run out of tiny offices.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Some artists choose to go from an independent label to a major label if given the opportunity, as major labels have considerably more power and financial means to promote and distribute products, sometimes increasing the chances of greater success.[[25]](#cite_note-Agnew2-25)

### Distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Distribution")]

There are a few ways an independent label may go about [distributing](/wiki/Music_distribution "Music distribution") its music. Some independent labels are owned by major labels, who carry out the distribution for them. Other labels instead go through independent distributors.[[22]](#cite_note-:02-22) Many current artists use their own resources to produce, record, market and release music through [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify"), [SoundCloud](/wiki/SoundCloud "SoundCloud"), and other streaming platforms with [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") in a direct, [do-it-yourself](/wiki/Do_it_yourself "Do it yourself") manner allowing creative distribution.[[26]](#cite_note-26) There is the potential for artists to gain large numbers of streams on Spotify if their music is included in certain popular playlists or has gained traction on [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

For both independent and major labels, the label generally owns the copyright to the sound recording. Artists who maintain their copyrights usually must sacrifice other parts of their deal, and must give the label a temporary license to the recordings.[[22]](#cite_note-:02-22)

### Contracts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Contracts")]

An [advance](/wiki/Advance_against_royalties "Advance against royalties") is a pre-payment of royalties from the label for the artist to record the album; it is paid back through the album's royalties.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelloggn.d.Advances_and_Recording_Budgets-28) Independent labels generally give out much smaller advances than major labels, if any.[[22]](#cite_note-:02-22)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelloggn.d.Advances_and_Recording_Budgets-28) Additionally, some independent labels will cover an album's recording costs instead of proving a set dollar amount as an advance. One advantage of smaller advances is that artists have less to pay back, and therefore can begin to profit quicker.[[22]](#cite_note-:02-22)

There are a number of ways that an independent label may structure their contract. Some independent labels have contracts that are essentially equivalent to major label deals.[[22]](#cite_note-:02-22) On a major label, a typical royalty rate (what the artist takes) is 13% to 16%;[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelloggn.d.Advances_and_Recording_Budgets-28) however, some independent labels offer 50-50 splits, which functions more as a partnership.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelloggn.d.Royalties-29)[[22]](#cite_note-:02-22) One issue is that artists often forgo their [mechanical royalties](/wiki/Royalty_payment "Royalty payment") in 50-50 deals,[[22]](#cite_note-:02-22) and it can be more difficult to recoup the advance, meaning it takes longer to turn a profit.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelloggn.d.Advances_and_Recording_Budgets-28) Some labels forgo a formal contract altogether, and their deals include few restrictions.[[22]](#cite_note-:02-22)

## Styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Styles")]

Independent music is a broad category that is made up of distinct subgenres with influences from various other genres.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

### Indie pop

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Indie pop")]

Main article: [Indie pop](/wiki/Indie_pop "Indie pop")

See also: [Bedroom pop](/wiki/Bedroom_pop "Bedroom pop")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Elliott_Smith.jpg/250px-Elliott_Smith.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elliott_Smith.jpg)

[Indie pop](/wiki/Indie_pop "Indie pop") musician [Elliott Smith](/wiki/Elliott_Smith "Elliott Smith")

[Indie pop](/wiki/Indie_pop "Indie pop") is a style of pop music that originally grew out of British [post-punk](/wiki/Post-punk "Post-punk") in the late 1970s.[[30]](#cite_note-:8-30)[[31]](#cite_note-:9-31) Indie pop was one of the first independent music genres, and was initially synonymous with "indie".[[31]](#cite_note-:9-31) Indie pop is characterized by a focus on [melody](/wiki/Melody "Melody"), arrangements, and [harmony](/wiki/Harmony "Harmony"), with less angst and [distortion](/wiki/Distortion_(music) "Distortion (music)") as compared to [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock").[[30]](#cite_note-:8-30)[[32]](#cite_note-:10-32) It features the homemade intimacy commonly found in independent music.[[30]](#cite_note-:8-30) Notable subgenres include [chamber pop](/wiki/Chamber_pop "Chamber pop"), which adds lush [chamber orchestration](/wiki/Chamber_music "Chamber music"), and [twee pop](/wiki/Twee_pop "Twee pop"), which features "primitive simplicity".[[32]](#cite_note-:10-32)

### Indie rock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Indie rock")]

Main article: [Indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock")

See also: [Slacker rock](/wiki/Slacker_rock "Slacker rock") and [shoegaze](/wiki/Shoegaze "Shoegaze")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/The_Strokes_live_collage_2019%E2%80%932020.jpg/330px-The_Strokes_live_collage_2019%E2%80%932020.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Strokes_live_collage_2019%E2%80%932020.jpg)

[Indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") band [the Strokes](/wiki/The_Strokes "The Strokes")

[Indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") (also referred to as simply "indie")[[33]](#cite_note-:11-33)[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) is a style of [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") and is one of the most popular independent music genres. It originally grew out of the [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock"), [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock"), and independent movements of the 1980s,[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[33]](#cite_note-:11-33) with local scenes emerging in many American cities and [college towns](/wiki/College_town "College town").[[34]](#cite_note-34) The New Zealand [Dunedin sound](/wiki/Dunedin_sound "Dunedin sound") of the 1970s and 80s was also influential in indie rock's development.[[35]](#cite_note-McConnell-35) By the 1990s, indie rock had separated from alternative rock and gained popularity in the mainstream,[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3) pushed along by the popularity of Seattle's [grunge](/wiki/Grunge "Grunge") scene, especially [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)").[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[33]](#cite_note-:11-33) Notable artists of the 2000s included [the Strokes](/wiki/The_Strokes "The Strokes"), [Yeah Yeah Yeahs](/wiki/Yeah_Yeah_Yeahs "Yeah Yeah Yeahs"), and the [Arctic Monkeys](/wiki/Arctic_Monkeys "Arctic Monkeys"),[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) while some of the 2010s included [the 1975](/wiki/The_1975 "The 1975") and [Vampire Weekend](/wiki/Vampire_Weekend "Vampire Weekend").[[33]](#cite_note-:11-33) Indie rock went onto inspire a multitude of subgenres and derivative styles, such as [dream pop](/wiki/Dream_pop "Dream pop"), [noise pop](/wiki/Noise_pop "Noise pop"), [lo-fi](/wiki/Lo-fi_music "Lo-fi music"), [math rock](/wiki/Math_rock "Math rock"), [emo](/wiki/Emo "Emo"), and more.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)

### Indie folk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Indie folk")]

Main article: [Indie folk](/wiki/Indie_folk "Indie folk")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Glastonbury2022_%28112_of_413%29_%2852182452613%29_Cropped.jpg/250px-Glastonbury2022_%28112_of_413%29_%2852182452613%29_Cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glastonbury2022_(112_of_413)_(52182452613)_Cropped.jpg)

[Indie folk](/wiki/Indie_folk "Indie folk") musician [Phoebe Bridgers](/wiki/Phoebe_Bridgers "Phoebe Bridgers")

[Indie folk](/wiki/Indie_folk "Indie folk") is a style of [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") which originated in the 1990s with artists like [Elliott Smith](/wiki/Elliott_Smith "Elliott Smith") and [Will Oldham](/wiki/Will_Oldham "Will Oldham").[[36]](#cite_note-:7-36) The genre grew from [traditional](/wiki/Traditional_folk_music "Traditional folk music") and [contemporary folk](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music"), but took a distinctly independent approach inspired by indie rock.[[37]](#cite_note-about.com2-37) The genre gained further popularity and support in the 2000s from labels such as [Saddle Creek](/wiki/Saddle_Creek_Records "Saddle Creek Records"), [Barsuk](/wiki/Barsuk_Records "Barsuk Records"), and [Sub Pop](/wiki/Sub_Pop "Sub Pop").[[36]](#cite_note-:7-36) Notable 21st century indie folk artists include [Fleet Foxes](/wiki/Fleet_Foxes "Fleet Foxes"), [Bon Iver](/wiki/Bon_Iver "Bon Iver"), [Great Lake Swimmers](/wiki/Great_Lake_Swimmers "Great Lake Swimmers"), [Sufjan Stevens](/wiki/Sufjan_Stevens "Sufjan Stevens"), and [Phoebe Bridgers](/wiki/Phoebe_Bridgers "Phoebe Bridgers").[[37]](#cite_note-about.com2-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) Indie folk is distinguished by its [acoustic instrumentation](/wiki/Acoustic_music "Acoustic music") – and often consists of just vocals and [acoustic guitar](/wiki/Acoustic_guitar "Acoustic guitar") – although some artists experiment with more diverse instrumentation. As well, indie folk artists are often [singer-songwriters](/wiki/Singer-songwriter "Singer-songwriter").[[37]](#cite_note-about.com2-37)

### Indie electronic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_music&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Indie electronic")]

Main article: [Indie electronic](/wiki/Indie_electronic "Indie electronic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/PostalService_color300dpi_by_Brian_Tamborello.jpg/330px-PostalService_color300dpi_by_Brian_Tamborello.jpg)](/wiki/File:PostalService_color300dpi_by_Brian_Tamborello.jpg)

[Indie electronic](/wiki/Indie_electronic "Indie electronic") band [the Postal Service](/wiki/The_Postal_Service "The Postal Service")

[Indie electronic](/wiki/Indie_electronic "Indie electronic"), also known as indietronica, is a broad categorization of music that combines independent and [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") styles.[[40]](#cite_note-:4-40)[[41]](#cite_note-:5-41) It is not considered a scene or movement, and often combines influences from a variety of genres.[[41]](#cite_note-:5-41) It has origins in the 1990s, with artists like [Stereolab](/wiki/Stereolab "Stereolab"), [Arab Strap](/wiki/Arab_Strap "Arab Strap"), and [Disco Inferno](/wiki/Disco_Inferno_(band) "Disco Inferno (band)") contributing to the style.[[40]](#cite_note-:4-40)[[41]](#cite_note-:5-41) Indietronica largely grew in popularity in the 2000s, with the rising accessibility to [home recording](/wiki/Home_recording "Home recording") and [software synthesizers](/wiki/Software_synthesizer "Software synthesizer").[[40]](#cite_note-:4-40) Influential artists of this era include [Hot Chip](/wiki/Hot_Chip "Hot Chip"), [Metronomy](/wiki/Metronomy "Metronomy"), and [the Postal Service](/wiki/The_Postal_Service "The Postal Service"). Some 2010s artists achieved wider success with their music, for example, [James Blake](/wiki/James_Blake_(musician) "James Blake (musician)") and [the xx](/wiki/The_xx "The xx").[[42]](#cite_note-:6-42) Indietronica artists usually release their music on independent labels, with examples including [Sub Pop](/wiki/Sub_Pop "Sub Pop"), [Warp](/wiki/Warp_(record_label) "Warp (record label)"), and [Ghostly International](/wiki/Ghostly_International "Ghostly International").[[40]](#cite_note-:4-40)
